articles.headline: Tragedy Strikes Indore: Contaminated Water Claims 14 Lives, Raises Public Health Concerns
articles.explainer:
• At least 14 people died and several were hospitalized after consuming contaminated water in Indore, Madhya Pradesh.
• The incident has sparked outrage and calls for immediate investigation into municipal water supply and sanitation standards.
• Public health experts highlight the recurring issue of water-borne diseases and the urgent need for robust water quality monitoring systems.
• Authorities have initiated an inquiry and announced compensation for the victims, while local administration faces scrutiny over negligence.
• This incident underscores systemic failures in urban infrastructure and public health management, particularly in rapidly growing cities.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
Governance, transparency and accountability
Disaster and disaster management
Environmental pollution and degradation
prelimsPoints: Indore is a major city in Madhya Pradesh, known for its Swachh Survekshan rankings.
Common water-borne diseases include cholera, typhoid, dysentery, and giardiasis.
The National Health Mission (NHM) and Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) are key government initiatives related to health and water access.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the factors contributing to recurring incidents of contaminated water and their impact on public health.
Discuss the role of local self-governing bodies in ensuring safe drinking water and effective waste management.
Suggest comprehensive measures, including policy reforms and technological interventions, to prevent such tragedies and improve urban water infrastructure.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of a broken water pipe leading to contaminated water, with people suffering in the background.
articles.trivia: Indore has consistently been ranked as India's cleanest city under the Swachh Survekshan survey, making this incident particularly concerning for its public health image.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Inflationary Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at its latest meeting.
• The decision was driven by persistent inflationary pressures, particularly in food and core inflation, despite some moderation.
• RBI Governor highlighted the need for continued vigilance to anchor inflation expectations and support sustainable economic growth.
• Analysts suggest the central bank is balancing growth impulses with the imperative of price stability in a volatile global economic environment.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to withdrawing accommodation gradually to ensure inflation aligns with the target.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary policy
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate in India.
The primary objective of the MPC is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks in the event of any shortfall of funds.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and economic growth in the current global scenario.
Discuss the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side driven inflation in India.
Evaluate the implications of a 'status quo' monetary policy on various sectors of the Indian economy, including lending, investment, and consumption.
articles.imageDescription: RBI logo with graphs showing economic indicators in the background.
articles.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee was constituted in 2016 and comprises three members from the RBI and three external members appointed by the Government of India.

articles.headline: India-France Strategic Dialogue Focuses on Indo-Pacific Security and Defence Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• High-level delegations from India and France convened for their annual Strategic Dialogue in New Delhi.
• Discussions centered on enhancing cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region, reaffirming commitment to a free, open, and inclusive approach.
• Key areas of collaboration included defense technology transfer, joint military exercises, and space cooperation.
• Both nations emphasized strengthening multilateralism and addressing global challenges like climate change and counter-terrorism.
• The dialogue underscored the growing convergence of strategic interests between India and France.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India and France established their strategic partnership in 1998.
France is a key partner for India in defense, particularly for Rafale jets and submarine technology.
The Indo-Pacific concept emphasizes connectivity, security, and open trade routes in the region.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-France strategic partnership in promoting stability and security in the Indo-Pacific region.
Discuss the various pillars of cooperation between India and France, highlighting their mutual benefits and challenges.
To what extent can India's deepening ties with European powers like France contribute to its strategic autonomy and geopolitical objectives?
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and France crossed, symbolizing diplomatic relations.
articles.trivia: France was one of the first countries to support India's nuclear program after the 1998 Pokhran tests, demonstrating a strong strategic alignment.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite EOS-07
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched its latest Earth Observation Satellite, EOS-07, from Sriharikota.
• Carried by the Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV-C58), the satellite is designed for high-resolution imaging and data collection.
• EOS-07 will provide crucial data for applications in agriculture, forestry, disaster management, and urban planning.
• The mission further solidifies India's indigenous capabilities in space technology and its commitment to utilizing space for national development.
• Several co-passenger payloads, including student satellites, were also deployed in the mission.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Space
prelimsPoints: ISRO is the premier space agency of India.
PSLV is known as ISRO's 'workhorse' launch vehicle.
Earth Observation Satellites are crucial for monitoring climate change, natural resources, and environmental degradation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Earth Observation Satellites for India's national development and security.
Evaluate ISRO's role in promoting self-reliance and technological advancement in the space sector.
Examine the potential applications of data from EOS-07 in addressing critical challenges like food security and disaster preparedness.
articles.imageDescription: A rocket launching into space, with ISRO logo visible.
articles.trivia: ISRO holds the world record for launching the most satellites in a single mission (104 satellites in 2017).

articles.headline: Supreme Court Resumes Hearing on Petitions Challenging Electoral Bond Scheme
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India resumed its crucial hearing on a batch of petitions challenging the constitutional validity of the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• Petitioners argue the scheme promotes opacity in political funding and violates citizens' right to information.
• The government has defended the scheme, citing its aim to curb black money in elections and ensure donor anonymity.
• The court is examining the balance between donor privacy and the public's right to know the source of political funding.
• The outcome could significantly impact electoral reforms and political transparency in India.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Governance, transparency and accountability
prelimsPoints: The Electoral Bond Scheme was introduced in 2017 through the Finance Act, 2017.
Electoral bonds are interest-free bearer instruments issued by specified branches of the State Bank of India.

Only political parties registered under Section 29A of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, and which secured at least one percent of the votes polled in the last general election or assembly election, are eligible to receive electoral bonds.
mainsPoints: Analyze the arguments for and against the Electoral Bond Scheme in the context of electoral transparency and political funding in India.
Discuss the role of the judiciary in upholding constitutional principles related to citizens' rights and democratic processes.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for transparent political funding that can address the concerns of both donor anonymity and public accountability.
articles.imageDescription: Supreme Court of India building with a gavel in the foreground.
articles.trivia: The Electoral Bond Scheme was challenged by several NGOs and political parties, including the Association for Democratic Reforms (ADR).

articles.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission Shows Promising Progress in Pilot Projects
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy announced significant progress in the pilot projects under the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
• Several industrial clusters and academic institutions are demonstrating viable green hydrogen production and application technologies.
• The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing to energy security and decarbonization.
• Focus areas include developing cost-effective electrolyzers, creating demand for green hydrogen in industries like fertilizers and refining, and establishing a robust regulatory framework.
• This initiative is critical for India to achieve its net-zero emissions target by 2070.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen using renewable electricity (electrolysis).
Grey hydrogen is produced from natural gas, and blue hydrogen is produced from natural gas with carbon capture technology.
India aims to achieve net-zero emissions by 2070.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of green hydrogen as a clean energy source for India and the challenges in its large-scale adoption.
Evaluate the economic and environmental benefits of the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
What policy interventions and technological advancements are required to establish India as a global leader in green hydrogen production and utilization?
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen plant with wind turbines and solar panels.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, but it rarely exists freely on Earth and must be separated from other elements.

articles.headline: Government Reviews PM-POSHAN Scheme, Focuses on Nutritional Outcomes
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a review meeting for the PM-POSHAN (Pradhan Mantri Poshan Shakti Nirman) Scheme.
• The review focused on improving the nutritional outcomes of school children and ensuring the quality and timely delivery of meals.
• Challenges related to logistics, infrastructure, and community participation were discussed, with new guidelines proposed.
• The scheme, which replaced the Mid-Day Meal Scheme, aims for holistic nutrition and health for children in government and government-aided schools.
• Emphasized the integration of local produce and traditional dietary practices to enhance nutritional value.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: PM-POSHAN Scheme was launched in 2021, replacing the National Programme of Mid-Day Meal in Schools (Mid-Day Meal Scheme).
It covers all school children studying in Classes I-VIII in government and government-aided schools.
The scheme also includes a provision for supplementary nutrition for children in aspirational districts and districts with a high prevalence of anemia.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the evolution of school meal programs in India, from the Mid-Day Meal Scheme to PM-POSHAN, in addressing child malnutrition.
Discuss the challenges in implementing large-scale nutritional programs and suggest measures to enhance their effectiveness and outreach.
How can community participation and local self-governance be leveraged to improve the quality and impact of the PM-POSHAN scheme?
articles.imageDescription: Children eating a meal in a school setting, symbolizing the PM-POSHAN scheme.
articles.trivia: The Mid-Day Meal Scheme, introduced in 1995, is one of the world's largest school feeding programmes.

articles.headline: PLI Scheme Boosts Domestic Manufacturing of Advanced Chemistry Cell Batteries
articles.explainer:
• The Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) Battery Storage received positive feedback from industry.
• Several major players have committed significant investments, leading to increased domestic production capabilities.
• This initiative is crucial for India's electric vehicle (EV) ecosystem and renewable energy storage solutions.
• The scheme aims to reduce India's import dependence on critical battery components and foster self-reliance.
• It is expected to create numerous jobs and establish India as a competitive manufacturing hub for ACC batteries.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: The PLI scheme for ACC batteries was approved in 2021 with an outlay of ₹18,100 crore.
ACC batteries are the new generation of advanced storage technologies that can store electric energy as electrochemical or chemical energy.
The scheme aims to establish a cumulative 50 GWh of ACC manufacturing capacity in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the role of the PLI scheme in boosting India's manufacturing capabilities and fostering self-reliance in critical sectors like ACC batteries.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for India in becoming a global hub for electric vehicle and battery manufacturing.
Evaluate the impact of domestic ACC battery production on India's energy security and environmental sustainability goals.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of various types of batteries and electric vehicle components.
articles.trivia: India is the third-largest automotive market in the world, making the transition to EVs and domestic battery production strategically important.

articles.headline: India Accelerates Border Infrastructure Development Along Northern Frontiers
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Defence announced accelerated development of critical infrastructure along India's northern borders.
• Projects include new roads, bridges, and strategic tunnels to improve troop mobility and logistics in challenging terrains.
• The move is aimed at bolstering national security and responding to evolving geopolitical dynamics in the region.
• Advanced construction technologies are being deployed to overcome geographical and climatic obstacles.
• This development is part of a broader strategy to enhance connectivity and deterrence capabilities in sensitive border areas.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Linkages between development and spread of extremism
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security
prelimsPoints: The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) is responsible for building and maintaining road networks in India's border areas.
The northern borders include regions like Ladakh, Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Sikkim, and Arunachal Pradesh.
Strategic tunnels, like the Atal Tunnel, significantly reduce travel time and provide all-weather connectivity.
mainsPoints: Examine the strategic imperative behind India's accelerated border infrastructure development, particularly along its northern frontiers.
Discuss the challenges involved in constructing infrastructure in high-altitude and difficult terrains and the innovative solutions being deployed.
Analyze the impact of improved border infrastructure on national security, regional development, and India's geopolitical standing.
articles.imageDescription: A newly constructed mountain road in a snowy, high-altitude border region.
articles.trivia: The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) operates under the Ministry of Defence and has constructed over 60,000 km of roads in difficult terrains.

articles.headline: Stakeholders Discuss Implementation Challenges of Digital Personal Data Protection Act
articles.explainer:
• A national consultation was held to discuss the practical challenges and roadmap for implementing the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023.
• Key concerns raised included the readiness of various industries, data localization requirements, and the capacity of the Data Protection Board.
• Experts emphasized the need for clear guidelines, robust enforcement mechanisms, and widespread public awareness campaigns.
• The Act aims to provide a framework for the processing of digital personal data in a manner that recognizes both the right of individuals to protect their personal data and the need to process such data for lawful purposes.
• Its effective implementation is crucial for safeguarding privacy rights in India's rapidly digitalizing economy.
gsSubTopics: Governance, transparency and accountability and institutional and other measures
Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, was passed by Parliament in August 2023.
It applies to the processing of digital personal data within India and offshore if it relates to offering goods/services to data principals in India.
The Act establishes a Data Protection Board of India to adjudicate non-compliance.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, and its potential impact on individual privacy and digital businesses in India.
Discuss the challenges in implementing a comprehensive data protection framework in a diverse and digitally evolving country like India.
Evaluate the balance struck by the Act between individual privacy rights and the legitimate interests of data processing entities and the state.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of digital data flowing through a secure network, with a lock icon.
articles.trivia: The DPDP Act is a culmination of years of deliberations, following the landmark Puttaswamy judgment (2017) which declared privacy a fundamental right.

articles.headline: Debate Continues Over Delimitation of Eco-Sensitive Zones in Western Ghats
articles.explainer:
• The long-standing debate over the demarcation of Eco-Sensitive Zones (ESZs) in the Western Ghats continues to involve state governments and environmental activists.
• Stakeholders are striving to balance conservation imperatives with the livelihood concerns of local communities.
• The recommendations of the Gadgil and Kasturirangan committees remain central to discussions, with states expressing reservations about their implementation.
• Environmental groups emphasize the urgent need to protect the biodiversity hotspot from unchecked development and exploitation.
• A consensus on the final boundaries and regulatory framework for ESZs is crucial for the ecological health of the region.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Salient features of world’s physical geography
prelimsPoints: The Western Ghats is one of the eight 'hottest hotspots' of biological diversity in the world.
The Gadgil Committee (Western Ghats Ecology Expert Panel - WGEEP) report was submitted in 2011.

The Kasturirangan Committee (High-Level Working Group - HLWG) report, submitted in 2013, proposed a more pragmatic approach than Gadgil, identifying a smaller area as Ecologically Sensitive Area (ESA).
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological significance of the Western Ghats and the threats it faces from developmental activities.
Compare and contrast the recommendations of the Gadgil and Kasturirangan committees regarding the conservation of the Western Ghats.
Analyze the challenges in achieving a balance between environmental conservation and the developmental aspirations of local communities in ecologically sensitive regions.
articles.imageDescription: Lush green mountains and forests of the Western Ghats.
articles.trivia: The Western Ghats are older than the Himalayas and are home to at least 325 globally threatened species.

articles.headline: India Advocates for Comprehensive Reforms in UN Peacekeeping Operations
articles.explainer:
• India reiterated its call for comprehensive reforms in United Nations Peacekeeping Operations at a high-level UN forum.
• Emphasized the need for clear and realistic mandates, better resources, and enhanced safety for peacekeepers.
• India, a major troop-contributing country, advocated for greater representation of troop contributors in UN decision-making processes.
• The discussions also touched upon adapting peacekeeping to modern challenges, including asymmetric threats and cyber warfare.
• India underscored its commitment to UN peacekeeping while pushing for necessary structural and operational improvements.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: India is one of the largest troop-contributing countries to UN peacekeeping missions.
UN Peacekeeping operations are guided by three basic principles: consent of the parties, impartiality, and non-use of force except in self-defence and defence of the mandate.
The UN Security Council authorizes peacekeeping missions.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's historical contribution and evolving role in UN Peacekeeping Operations.
Discuss the major challenges faced by UN peacekeeping missions in the contemporary global security landscape.
Evaluate India's proposals for reforms in UN Peacekeeping and their potential impact on global peace and security.
articles.imageDescription: UN peacekeepers in blue helmets standing in a conflict zone.
articles.trivia: More than 2,70,000 Indian peacekeepers have served in 49 of the 71 UN peacekeeping missions established so far.

articles.headline: National Mental Health Awareness Campaign Launched to Combat Stigma
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare launched a nationwide campaign to raise awareness about mental health and reduce associated stigma.
• The campaign emphasizes early identification, access to care, and the importance of a supportive environment.
• It features public service announcements, community outreach programs, and digital initiatives to reach a wider audience.
• The initiative aims to normalize conversations around mental health and encourage individuals to seek professional help.
• This is part of a broader strategy to strengthen mental healthcare infrastructure and services across the country.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
prelimsPoints: The National Mental Health Program (NMHP) was launched in 1982 to provide mental healthcare services.
Tele-MANAS, a toll-free mental health helpline, was launched in 2022 to provide 24/7 mental health services.
The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, aims to provide mental healthcare and services for persons with mental illness.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing burden of mental health issues in India and the societal factors contributing to the stigma associated with them.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government initiatives and awareness campaigns in addressing mental health challenges.
Suggest a multi-pronged approach involving policy, community engagement, and healthcare infrastructure to improve mental health outcomes in India.
articles.imageDescription: A silhouette of a person with a thought bubble showing a calm, serene mind, against a backdrop of diverse people.
articles.trivia: Globally, one in eight people live with a mental disorder, and mental health conditions account for 13% of the global burden of disease.

articles.headline: MSME Sector Shows Resilience, Seeks Further Policy Support for Growth
articles.explainer:
• The Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSME) sector continues to demonstrate resilience but calls for enhanced policy support.
• Industry bodies highlighted challenges such as access to credit, technology adoption, and market linkages.
• Government initiatives like the 'Udyam' portal and various credit guarantee schemes have provided some relief.
• The sector remains a crucial engine for employment generation and contributes significantly to India's GDP and exports.
• Further reforms are sought to streamline regulatory processes and foster a more competitive environment for MSMEs.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
keyTerms: Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises (CGTMSE)
prelimsPoints: MSMEs are defined based on investment in plant and machinery/equipment and turnover.
The Udyam Registration portal was launched in 2020 for formalizing MSMEs.
The MSME sector contributes approximately 30% to India's GDP and around 45% to manufacturing output.
mainsPoints: Examine the significance of the MSME sector in achieving inclusive growth and employment generation in India.
Discuss the key challenges faced by MSMEs in India and evaluate the effectiveness of government policies in addressing them.
Suggest reforms and strategies to further strengthen the MSME sector, enabling it to leverage global opportunities and contribute to 'Atmanirbhar Bharat'.
articles.imageDescription: A small factory building with gears and workers, symbolizing the MSME sector.
articles.trivia: The MSME Development Act was enacted in 2006 to facilitate the promotion, development, and enhancement of the competitiveness of MSMEs.

articles.headline: India Boosts Research and Development in Quantum Computing Technologies
articles.explainer:
• The Department of Science & Technology announced new funding and collaborative initiatives to accelerate R&D in quantum computing.
• Focus areas include quantum hardware development, quantum algorithms, and quantum communication.
• The National Quantum Mission aims to position India as a leading nation in quantum technologies and applications.
• Collaborations between academic institutions, industry, and government labs are being prioritized to foster innovation.
• Quantum computing holds immense potential for breakthroughs in fields like drug discovery, materials science, and cryptography.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
prelimsPoints: Quantum computers use principles of quantum mechanics (superposition, entanglement) to process information.
Unlike classical bits, which are either 0 or 1, qubits can exist in multiple states simultaneously.
The National Quantum Mission was approved in 2023 with a total outlay of ₹6,003.65 crore for 2023-24 to 2030-31.
mainsPoints: Explain the fundamental principles of quantum computing and its potential to revolutionize various sectors.
Discuss the objectives and strategic importance of India's National Quantum Mission in the global race for technological leadership.
Analyze the challenges and ethical considerations associated with the development and deployment of quantum technologies.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract representation of quantum particles and connections, symbolizing quantum computing.
articles.trivia: The term 'quantum supremacy' refers to the point where a quantum computer can perform a task that a classical supercomputer cannot perform in any feasible amount of time.

articles.headline: Government Emphasizes Strengthening Panchayati Raj Institutions for Grassroots Development
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Panchayati Raj held a conference emphasizing the critical role of Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) in local governance and rural development.
• Discussions focused on empowering PRIs through increased financial devolution, capacity building, and technological integration.
• Challenges such as inadequate funding, lack of trained personnel, and limited functional autonomy were highlighted.
• The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act envisioned PRIs as institutions of self-governance, crucial for decentralized planning and implementation of welfare schemes.
• Efforts are underway to leverage PRIs for achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) at the local level.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
prelimsPoints: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions.
It mandated the establishment of a three-tier system of Panchayats (Gram, Block, District levels).
The State Election Commission and State Finance Commission are crucial for the functioning of PRIs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional provisions for Panchayati Raj Institutions and their significance in India's democratic decentralization.
Discuss the major challenges hindering the effective functioning of PRIs and their capacity to drive grassroots development.
Suggest measures to strengthen PRIs, including financial autonomy, administrative capacity building, and enhanced citizen participation.
articles.imageDescription: A group of villagers sitting together in a Gram Sabha meeting, symbolizing local governance.
articles.trivia: Rajasthan was the first state to establish Panchayati Raj in 1959, although it gained constitutional status much later.

articles.headline: Major Cities Review Urban Flooding Preparedness Amidst Climate Change Concerns
articles.explainer:
• Several major Indian cities conducted reviews of their urban flooding preparedness plans in anticipation of changing weather patterns.
• The reviews focused on improving drainage systems, enhancing early warning mechanisms, and strengthening disaster response teams.
• Unplanned urbanization, encroachment on natural water bodies, and climate change-induced extreme rainfall are identified as key causes of urban flooding.
• Municipal corporations are collaborating with national disaster management authorities to develop resilient urban infrastructure.
• Public awareness campaigns are also being initiated to educate citizens on preventive measures and emergency protocols.
gsSubTopics: Disaster and disaster management

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: Urban flooding is distinct from rural flooding, primarily caused by rapid urbanization and inadequate drainage.
The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) provides guidelines for urban flood management.
Smart City Mission aims to promote sustainable and inclusive cities, including resilient infrastructure.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multi-faceted causes of urban flooding in India and its socio-economic consequences.
Discuss the strategies and policy interventions required to enhance urban resilience to climate change-induced extreme weather events.
Evaluate the role of urban local bodies and citizen participation in effective urban flood preparedness and management.
articles.imageDescription: A flooded city street with cars submerged in water, showing the impact of urban flooding.
articles.trivia: Chennai, Mumbai, and Bengaluru are among the Indian cities most vulnerable to urban flooding due to their coastal location and rapid, often unplanned, urbanization.

articles.headline: Ministry of Culture Launches Scheme to Revitalize Traditional Indian Arts and Crafts
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Culture introduced a new national scheme aimed at preserving and promoting India's rich heritage of traditional arts and crafts.
• The scheme provides financial assistance, training, and market linkage support to artisans and craftspeople.
• Focus areas include reviving endangered art forms, empowering rural artisans, and leveraging technology for wider outreach.
• It seeks to enhance the socio-economic well-being of artisans and promote cultural tourism.
• This initiative aligns with the 'Vocal for Local' campaign and aims to showcase India's cultural diversity globally.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
prelimsPoints: India has a diverse range of traditional arts and crafts, including pottery, weaving, painting, and sculpting.
Geographical Indication (GI) tags help protect the intellectual property rights of traditional products specific to a region.
The 'Vocal for Local' initiative promotes indigenous products and local businesses.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of traditional Indian arts and crafts in preserving cultural identity and promoting economic livelihoods.
Analyze the challenges faced by artisans in the contemporary market and the impact of globalization on traditional crafts.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government schemes and policies in revitalizing traditional arts and crafts and suggest further measures for their sustainable growth.
articles.imageDescription: An artisan meticulously carving a traditional wooden craft.
articles.trivia: The 'Kumbh Mela' is recognized by UNESCO as an Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

articles.headline: India Unveils Enhanced Maritime Security Strategy for Indian Ocean Region
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Defence released an updated maritime security strategy, emphasizing India's role as a net security provider in the Indian Ocean Region (IOR).
• The strategy focuses on strengthening naval capabilities, enhancing surveillance, and fostering regional partnerships.
• It addresses emerging threats such as maritime terrorism, piracy, illegal fishing, and challenges to freedom of navigation.
• India aims to promote a rules-based order and ensure peace and stability in its strategic maritime neighborhood.
• Collaborative initiatives with littoral states for capacity building and joint exercises are central to the new approach.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security
India and its neighbourhood- relations
keyTerms: SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region)
prelimsPoints: The Indian Ocean Region is strategically vital, connecting major global trade routes.
India's SAGAR doctrine emphasizes security and growth for all in the region.
The Indian Navy is the primary agency responsible for India's maritime security.
mainsPoints: Analyze the geopolitical significance of the Indian Ocean Region for India and the evolving maritime security challenges it faces.
Discuss the key pillars of India's enhanced maritime security strategy and its implications for regional stability.
Evaluate the role of regional cooperation and multilateral initiatives in addressing non-traditional maritime threats in the IOR.
articles.imageDescription: An Indian naval ship patrolling the open sea, with a map of the Indian Ocean in the background.
articles.trivia: India has a coastline of over 7,500 km and a vast Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) of over 2 million sq km, making maritime security crucial.

articles.headline: National Education Policy Implementation: Focus on Foundational Literacy and Numeracy
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a high-level review of the implementation progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• Special emphasis was placed on strengthening foundational literacy and numeracy (FLN) skills among young learners.
• Challenges in teacher training, curriculum development, and digital infrastructure were discussed, with remedial actions proposed.
• The NEP 2020 envisions a holistic, flexible, multidisciplinary education system aligned with 21st-century needs.
• States were urged to expedite reforms, including early childhood care and education, and skill integration.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the National Policy on Education of 1986.
The 5+3+3+4 curricular structure is a key reform proposed by NEP 2020.
The NIPUN Bharat Mission aims to achieve universal foundational literacy and numeracy for all children by the end of Grade 3 by 2026-27.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 in reforming India's education system.
Discuss the challenges in implementing a large-scale education policy across diverse states and socio-economic contexts.
Evaluate the significance of foundational literacy and numeracy in achieving equitable and quality education for all, as envisioned by NEP 2020.
articles.imageDescription: Children studying in a modern classroom, with a teacher assisting them.
articles.trivia: India's first National Policy on Education was formulated in 1968, based on the recommendations of the Kothari Commission (1964-66).

articles.headline: Government Promotes Climate-Resilient Agriculture Practices to Boost Farmer Income
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare launched new initiatives to promote climate-resilient agriculture across vulnerable regions.
• Focus areas include developing drought-resistant crops, efficient water management techniques, and agro-advisory services.
• The program aims to enhance food security, mitigate the impact of climate change on agriculture, and improve farmer livelihoods.
• Farmers are being encouraged to adopt sustainable practices like organic farming, crop diversification, and precision agriculture.
• Subsidies and technical support are being provided to facilitate the transition to climate-smart farming methods.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
keyTerms: National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA)
prelimsPoints: India is highly vulnerable to climate change impacts, especially in its agriculture sector.
The National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA) is a major project of ICAR.
Organic farming and Zero Budget Natural Farming (ZBNF) are practices promoted for sustainable agriculture.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges posed by climate change to Indian agriculture and its implications for food security and farmer livelihoods.
Analyze the various climate-resilient agriculture practices and technologies being promoted by the government.
Suggest policy measures and institutional reforms to foster widespread adoption of sustainable and climate-smart farming methods in India.
articles.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with advanced irrigation systems under a bright sky.
articles.trivia: India is one of the world's largest agricultural producers, but also one of the most climate-vulnerable.

articles.headline: E-Governance Initiatives Streamline Citizen Services and Enhance Transparency
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) highlighted the success of various e-governance initiatives in improving citizen service delivery.
• Digital platforms for public services, grievance redressal, and information dissemination have seen increased adoption.
• The initiatives aim to reduce corruption, enhance transparency, and improve efficiency in government operations.
• Focus is also on bridging the digital divide and ensuring accessibility of services to rural and marginalized populations.
• Further integration of AI and blockchain technologies is being explored to make governance more proactive and citizen-centric.
gsSubTopics: Governance, transparency and accountability and institutional and other measures
E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures
prelimsPoints: Digital India program was launched in 2015 to transform India into a digitally empowered society and knowledge economy.
UMANG (Unified Mobile Application for New-age Governance) provides access to various government services on a single platform.
The National e-Governance Plan (NeGP) aims to make all government services accessible to the common man.
mainsPoints: Analyze the benefits and challenges of e-governance initiatives in improving public service delivery and fostering transparency in India.
Discuss how digital technologies can be leveraged to bridge the digital divide and ensure inclusive access to government services.
Suggest measures to enhance the effectiveness, security, and citizen adoption of e-governance platforms in a rapidly evolving digital landscape.
articles.imageDescription: A hand holding a smartphone displaying various government service icons, with people interacting digitally in the background.
articles.trivia: India's Aadhaar program is the world's largest biometric ID system, facilitating numerous e-governance services.

articles.headline: India-Central Asia Summit Reinforces Regional Connectivity and Economic Ties
articles.explainer:
• India hosted the second India-Central Asia Summit, bringing together leaders from five Central Asian republics.
• The summit focused on enhancing regional connectivity through infrastructure projects and trade facilitation measures.
• Discussions included cooperation in energy, security, culture, and people-to-people exchanges.
• India reiterated its commitment to strengthening economic partnerships and strategic dialogue with Central Asian nations.
• The leaders adopted a joint declaration, emphasizing a shared vision for peace, prosperity, and stability in the region.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The five Central Asian Republics are Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan.
India's 'Connect Central Asia' policy was launched in 2012.
The Chabahar Port in Iran is crucial for India's connectivity to Afghanistan and Central Asia, bypassing Pakistan.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of Central Asian Republics for India's energy security, connectivity, and geopolitical interests.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in enhancing trade and infrastructure linkages between India and Central Asia.
Evaluate India's 'Extended Neighborhood Policy' and its role in fostering deeper engagement with the Central Asian region.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Central Asian countries arranged together, symbolizing a summit.
articles.trivia: The first India-Central Asia Summit was held in January 2022, marking the 30th anniversary of diplomatic relations between India and the Central Asian countries.

highlights: Indore tragedy: 14 dead due to contaminated water, sparking nationwide concerns over public health and municipal accountability.
RBI maintains status quo on repo rate, signaling continued vigilance on inflation amidst global economic uncertainties.
India-France Strategic Dialogue strengthens cooperation in Indo-Pacific security, defense, and advanced technologies.
ISRO successfully launches EOS-07, bolstering India's earth observation capabilities for agriculture, disaster management, and urban planning.
Supreme Court resumes hearing on Electoral Bond Scheme, with potential implications for political funding transparency in India.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements regarding the Electoral Bond Scheme is/are correct?
1. The scheme was introduced through the Finance Act, 2017.
2. Only political parties registered under Section 29A of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, are eligible to receive electoral bonds.
3. Electoral bonds are interest-bearing instruments issued by specified branches of the State Bank of India.

Select the correct option:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The Electoral Bond Scheme was indeed introduced in 2017 through the Finance Act, 2017. Statement 2 is correct: Only eligible political parties (registered under Section 29A of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, and securing at least one percent of the votes polled in the last general election or assembly election) can receive electoral bonds. Statement 3 is incorrect: Electoral bonds are interest-free bearer instruments, not interest-bearing.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements about Green Hydrogen:
1. Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using electricity generated from fossil fuels.
2. India aims to become a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
3. Grey hydrogen is produced from natural gas with carbon capture technology.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using renewable electricity (electrolysis), not fossil fuels. Statement 2 is correct: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen. Statement 3 is incorrect: Grey hydrogen is produced from natural gas without carbon capture. Blue hydrogen is produced from natural gas with carbon capture technology.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following committees/panels are associated with the conservation of the Western Ghats?
1. Gadgil Committee
2. Kasturirangan Committee
3. Kothari Commission

Select the correct option:
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Gadgil Committee (Western Ghats Ecology Expert Panel - WGEEP) and the Kasturirangan Committee (High-Level Working Group - HLWG) are both associated with recommendations for the conservation and protection of the Western Ghats. The Kothari Commission (1964-66) was related to education reform in India.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the PM-POSHAN Scheme, consider the following statements:
1. It replaced the National Programme of Mid-Day Meal in Schools.
2. It covers all school children studying in Classes I-XII in government and government-aided schools.
3. The scheme includes provision for supplementary nutrition for children in aspirational districts.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: PM-POSHAN Scheme was launched in 2021, replacing the Mid-Day Meal Scheme. Statement 2 is incorrect: It covers children studying in Classes I-VIII, not up to Class XII. Statement 3 is correct: The scheme includes a provision for supplementary nutrition for children in aspirational districts and districts with a high prevalence of anemia.

prelimsMocks.question: The term 'Qubit' is most closely associated with:
prelimsMocks.explanation: A 'Qubit' (quantum bit) is the basic unit of information in quantum computing, analogous to a bit in classical computing. Unlike classical bits, which are either 0 or 1, a qubit can exist in a superposition of both states simultaneously.

mainsMocks.question: Analyze the systemic failures highlighted by the Indore contaminated water tragedy and suggest comprehensive governance reforms to prevent such public health crises in urban areas. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health; Governance, transparency and accountability.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the Indore incident as a critical public health failure. Discuss systemic issues like inadequate infrastructure, poor water quality monitoring, lack of accountability, and rapid unplanned urbanization. Suggest reforms: strengthening municipal bodies, investing in water infrastructure, enforcing quality standards, public participation, and leveraging technology for monitoring. Conclude with the importance of proactive governance for public health.

mainsMocks.question: Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in achieving its ambitious National Green Hydrogen Mission goals, particularly in the context of energy security and climate change mitigation. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment; Infrastructure: Energy; Science and Technology- developments.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by defining green hydrogen and the mission's objectives. Discuss challenges: high production costs, need for massive renewable energy expansion, infrastructure for storage and transport, and technological hurdles. Highlight opportunities: energy security, decarbonization of hard-to-abate sectors, export potential, and job creation. Conclude with policy recommendations for R&D, incentives, and international collaboration.

mainsMocks.question: Discuss the significance of India's enhanced maritime security strategy for the Indian Ocean Region (IOR). How does it address traditional and non-traditional threats, and what role do regional partnerships play in its implementation? (10 marks, 150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Security challenges and their management in border areas; GS2: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
mainsMocks.approach: Start by explaining IOR's strategic importance. Outline the strategy's pillars: naval capabilities, surveillance, regional partnerships. Address traditional threats (state-sponsored challenges) and non-traditional threats (piracy, terrorism, illegal fishing). Emphasize the role of initiatives like SAGAR and collaborative exercises with littoral states. Conclude on India's role as a net security provider for regional stability.

The RBI's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) comprises six members, three from RBI and three external members.

GS1.headline: Ministry of Culture Launches Scheme to Revitalize Traditional Indian Arts and Crafts
GS1.explainer:
• The Ministry of Culture introduced a new national scheme aimed at preserving and promoting India's rich heritage of traditional arts and crafts.
• The scheme provides financial assistance, training, and market linkage support to artisans and craftspeople.
• Focus areas include reviving endangered art forms, empowering rural artisans, and leveraging technology for wider outreach.
• It seeks to enhance the socio-economic well-being of artisans and promote cultural tourism.
• This initiative aligns with the 'Vocal for Local' campaign and aims to showcase India's cultural diversity globally.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
prelimsPoints: India has a diverse range of traditional arts and crafts, including pottery, weaving, painting, and sculpting.
Geographical Indication (GI) tags help protect the intellectual property rights of traditional products specific to a region.
The 'Vocal for Local' initiative promotes indigenous products and local businesses.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of traditional Indian arts and crafts in preserving cultural identity and promoting economic livelihoods.
Analyze the challenges faced by artisans in the contemporary market and the impact of globalization on traditional crafts.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government schemes and policies in revitalizing traditional arts and crafts and suggest further measures for their sustainable growth.
GS1.imageDescription: An artisan meticulously carving a traditional wooden craft.
GS1.trivia: The 'Kumbh Mela' is recognized by UNESCO as an Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

GS2.headline: Tragedy Strikes Indore: Contaminated Water Claims 14 Lives, Raises Public Health Concerns
GS2.explainer:
• At least 14 people died and several were hospitalized after consuming contaminated water in Indore, Madhya Pradesh.
• The incident has sparked outrage and calls for immediate investigation into municipal water supply and sanitation standards.
• Public health experts highlight the recurring issue of water-borne diseases and the urgent need for robust water quality monitoring systems.
• Authorities have initiated an inquiry and announced compensation for the victims, while local administration faces scrutiny over negligence.
• This incident underscores systemic failures in urban infrastructure and public health management, particularly in rapidly growing cities.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
Governance, transparency and accountability
Disaster and disaster management
Environmental pollution and degradation
prelimsPoints: Indore is a major city in Madhya Pradesh, known for its Swachh Survekshan rankings.
Common water-borne diseases include cholera, typhoid, dysentery, and giardiasis.
The National Health Mission (NHM) and Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) are key government initiatives related to health and water access.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the factors contributing to recurring incidents of contaminated water and their impact on public health.
Discuss the role of local self-governing bodies in ensuring safe drinking water and effective waste management.
Suggest comprehensive measures, including policy reforms and technological interventions, to prevent such tragedies and improve urban water infrastructure.
GS2.imageDescription: Illustration of a broken water pipe leading to contaminated water, with people suffering in the background.
GS2.trivia: Indore has consistently been ranked as India's cleanest city under the Swachh Survekshan survey, making this incident particularly concerning for its public health image.

GS2.headline: India-France Strategic Dialogue Focuses on Indo-Pacific Security and Defence Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• High-level delegations from India and France convened for their annual Strategic Dialogue in New Delhi.
• Discussions centered on enhancing cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region, reaffirming commitment to a free, open, and inclusive approach.
• Key areas of collaboration included defense technology transfer, joint military exercises, and space cooperation.
• Both nations emphasized strengthening multilateralism and addressing global challenges like climate change and counter-terrorism.
• The dialogue underscored the growing convergence of strategic interests between India and France.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India and France established their strategic partnership in 1998.
France is a key partner for India in defense, particularly for Rafale jets and submarine technology.
The Indo-Pacific concept emphasizes connectivity, security, and open trade routes in the region.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-France strategic partnership in promoting stability and security in the Indo-Pacific region.
Discuss the various pillars of cooperation between India and France, highlighting their mutual benefits and challenges.
To what extent can India's deepening ties with European powers like France contribute to its strategic autonomy and geopolitical objectives?
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and France crossed, symbolizing diplomatic relations.
GS2.trivia: France was one of the first countries to support India's nuclear program after the 1998 Pokhran tests, demonstrating a strong strategic alignment.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Resumes Hearing on Petitions Challenging Electoral Bond Scheme
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India resumed its crucial hearing on a batch of petitions challenging the constitutional validity of the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• Petitioners argue the scheme promotes opacity in political funding and violates citizens' right to information.
• The government has defended the scheme, citing its aim to curb black money in elections and ensure donor anonymity.
• The court is examining the balance between donor privacy and the public's right to know the source of political funding.
• The outcome could significantly impact electoral reforms and political transparency in India.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Governance, transparency and accountability
prelimsPoints: The Electoral Bond Scheme was introduced in 2017 through the Finance Act, 2017.
Electoral bonds are interest-free bearer instruments issued by specified branches of the State Bank of India.

Only political parties registered under Section 29A of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, and which secured at least one percent of the votes polled in the last general election or assembly election, are eligible to receive electoral bonds.
mainsPoints: Analyze the arguments for and against the Electoral Bond Scheme in the context of electoral transparency and political funding in India.
Discuss the role of the judiciary in upholding constitutional principles related to citizens' rights and democratic processes.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for transparent political funding that can address the concerns of both donor anonymity and public accountability.
GS2.imageDescription: Supreme Court of India building with a gavel in the foreground.
GS2.trivia: The Electoral Bond Scheme was challenged by several NGOs and political parties, including the Association for Democratic Reforms (ADR).

GS2.headline: Government Reviews PM-POSHAN Scheme, Focuses on Nutritional Outcomes
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a review meeting for the PM-POSHAN (Pradhan Mantri Poshan Shakti Nirman) Scheme.
• The review focused on improving the nutritional outcomes of school children and ensuring the quality and timely delivery of meals.
• Challenges related to logistics, infrastructure, and community participation were discussed, with new guidelines proposed.
• The scheme, which replaced the Mid-Day Meal Scheme, aims for holistic nutrition and health for children in government and government-aided schools.
• Emphasized the integration of local produce and traditional dietary practices to enhance nutritional value.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: PM-POSHAN Scheme was launched in 2021, replacing the National Programme of Mid-Day Meal in Schools (Mid-Day Meal Scheme).
It covers all school children studying in Classes I-VIII in government and government-aided schools.
The scheme also includes a provision for supplementary nutrition for children in aspirational districts and districts with a high prevalence of anemia.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the evolution of school meal programs in India, from the Mid-Day Meal Scheme to PM-POSHAN, in addressing child malnutrition.
Discuss the challenges in implementing large-scale nutritional programs and suggest measures to enhance their effectiveness and outreach.
How can community participation and local self-governance be leveraged to improve the quality and impact of the PM-POSHAN scheme?
GS2.imageDescription: Children eating a meal in a school setting, symbolizing the PM-POSHAN scheme.
GS2.trivia: The Mid-Day Meal Scheme, introduced in 1995, is one of the world's largest school feeding programmes.

GS2.headline: Stakeholders Discuss Implementation Challenges of Digital Personal Data Protection Act
GS2.explainer:
• A national consultation was held to discuss the practical challenges and roadmap for implementing the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023.
• Key concerns raised included the readiness of various industries, data localization requirements, and the capacity of the Data Protection Board.
• Experts emphasized the need for clear guidelines, robust enforcement mechanisms, and widespread public awareness campaigns.
• The Act aims to provide a framework for the processing of digital personal data in a manner that recognizes both the right of individuals to protect their personal data and the need to process such data for lawful purposes.
• Its effective implementation is crucial for safeguarding privacy rights in India's rapidly digitalizing economy.
gsSubTopics: Governance, transparency and accountability and institutional and other measures
Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, was passed by Parliament in August 2023.
It applies to the processing of digital personal data within India and offshore if it relates to offering goods/services to data principals in India.
The Act establishes a Data Protection Board of India to adjudicate non-compliance.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, and its potential impact on individual privacy and digital businesses in India.
Discuss the challenges in implementing a comprehensive data protection framework in a diverse and digitally evolving country like India.
Evaluate the balance struck by the Act between individual privacy rights and the legitimate interests of data processing entities and the state.
GS2.imageDescription: Illustration of digital data flowing through a secure network, with a lock icon.
GS2.trivia: The DPDP Act is a culmination of years of deliberations, following the landmark Puttaswamy judgment (2017) which declared privacy a fundamental right.

GS2.headline: India Advocates for Comprehensive Reforms in UN Peacekeeping Operations
GS2.explainer:
• India reiterated its call for comprehensive reforms in United Nations Peacekeeping Operations at a high-level UN forum.
• Emphasized the need for clear and realistic mandates, better resources, and enhanced safety for peacekeepers.
• India, a major troop-contributing country, advocated for greater representation of troop contributors in UN decision-making processes.
• The discussions also touched upon adapting peacekeeping to modern challenges, including asymmetric threats and cyber warfare.
• India underscored its commitment to UN peacekeeping while pushing for necessary structural and operational improvements.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: India is one of the largest troop-contributing countries to UN peacekeeping missions.
UN Peacekeeping operations are guided by three basic principles: consent of the parties, impartiality, and non-use of force except in self-defence and defence of the mandate.
The UN Security Council authorizes peacekeeping missions.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's historical contribution and evolving role in UN Peacekeeping Operations.
Discuss the major challenges faced by UN peacekeeping missions in the contemporary global security landscape.
Evaluate India's proposals for reforms in UN Peacekeeping and their potential impact on global peace and security.
GS2.imageDescription: UN peacekeepers in blue helmets standing in a conflict zone.
GS2.trivia: More than 2,70,000 Indian peacekeepers have served in 49 of the 71 UN peacekeeping missions established so far.

GS2.headline: National Mental Health Awareness Campaign Launched to Combat Stigma
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare launched a nationwide campaign to raise awareness about mental health and reduce associated stigma.
• The campaign emphasizes early identification, access to care, and the importance of a supportive environment.
• It features public service announcements, community outreach programs, and digital initiatives to reach a wider audience.
• The initiative aims to normalize conversations around mental health and encourage individuals to seek professional help.
• This is part of a broader strategy to strengthen mental healthcare infrastructure and services across the country.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
prelimsPoints: The National Mental Health Program (NMHP) was launched in 1982 to provide mental healthcare services.
Tele-MANAS, a toll-free mental health helpline, was launched in 2022 to provide 24/7 mental health services.
The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, aims to provide mental healthcare and services for persons with mental illness.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing burden of mental health issues in India and the societal factors contributing to the stigma associated with them.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government initiatives and awareness campaigns in addressing mental health challenges.
Suggest a multi-pronged approach involving policy, community engagement, and healthcare infrastructure to improve mental health outcomes in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A silhouette of a person with a thought bubble showing a calm, serene mind, against a backdrop of diverse people.
GS2.trivia: Globally, one in eight people live with a mental disorder, and mental health conditions account for 13% of the global burden of disease.

GS2.headline: Government Emphasizes Strengthening Panchayati Raj Institutions for Grassroots Development
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Panchayati Raj held a conference emphasizing the critical role of Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) in local governance and rural development.
• Discussions focused on empowering PRIs through increased financial devolution, capacity building, and technological integration.
• Challenges such as inadequate funding, lack of trained personnel, and limited functional autonomy were highlighted.
• The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act envisioned PRIs as institutions of self-governance, crucial for decentralized planning and implementation of welfare schemes.
• Efforts are underway to leverage PRIs for achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) at the local level.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
prelimsPoints: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions.
It mandated the establishment of a three-tier system of Panchayats (Gram, Block, District levels).
The State Election Commission and State Finance Commission are crucial for the functioning of PRIs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional provisions for Panchayati Raj Institutions and their significance in India's democratic decentralization.
Discuss the major challenges hindering the effective functioning of PRIs and their capacity to drive grassroots development.
Suggest measures to strengthen PRIs, including financial autonomy, administrative capacity building, and enhanced citizen participation.
GS2.imageDescription: A group of villagers sitting together in a Gram Sabha meeting, symbolizing local governance.
GS2.trivia: Rajasthan was the first state to establish Panchayati Raj in 1959, although it gained constitutional status much later.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy Implementation: Focus on Foundational Literacy and Numeracy
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a high-level review of the implementation progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• Special emphasis was placed on strengthening foundational literacy and numeracy (FLN) skills among young learners.
• Challenges in teacher training, curriculum development, and digital infrastructure were discussed, with remedial actions proposed.
• The NEP 2020 envisions a holistic, flexible, multidisciplinary education system aligned with 21st-century needs.
• States were urged to expedite reforms, including early childhood care and education, and skill integration.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the National Policy on Education of 1986.
The 5+3+3+4 curricular structure is a key reform proposed by NEP 2020.
The NIPUN Bharat Mission aims to achieve universal foundational literacy and numeracy for all children by the end of Grade 3 by 2026-27.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 in reforming India's education system.
Discuss the challenges in implementing a large-scale education policy across diverse states and socio-economic contexts.
Evaluate the significance of foundational literacy and numeracy in achieving equitable and quality education for all, as envisioned by NEP 2020.
GS2.imageDescription: Children studying in a modern classroom, with a teacher assisting them.
GS2.trivia: India's first National Policy on Education was formulated in 1968, based on the recommendations of the Kothari Commission (1964-66).
